core_intuitions: MoE decouples the number of parameters (capacity/knowledge, = TOTAL) from the FLOPs per token (cost/latency, = ACTIVE): a bank of E experts + a router that runs only k<<E of them, so the model 'contains' E FFNs but 'pays' for k.

The three-way resource split is the whole bet: quality tracks TOTAL params, latency/FLOPs track ACTIVE params, and memory tracks TOTAL (every expert must stay resident because any token can route anywhere) - which is why you reason with the total-to-active RATIO, never a single number.

The router is a tiny linear gate -> per-expert logits -> top-k select (the discrete, non-differentiable step) -> softmax gate weights, and the order matters: softmax-then-topk (weights don't sum to 1) vs topk-then-softmax (renormalized, stable magnitude).

Load balancing is make-or-break: a sparse router collapses via a rich-get-richer feedback loop (favored expert -> more tokens -> more gradient -> improves faster -> even more tokens); the aux loss breaks it with a hard dispatch-count (direction, non-diff) times a soft probability (gradient), and expert-choice routing balances by construction.

Capacity is a fixed-size rectangular buffer per expert (GPUs need static tensors): capacity = ceil(Cf * Nk/E); overflow tokens are DROPPED (pass through on the residual, a quality cost) and underflow is PADDED with zeros (a compute cost) - the capacity factor trades these two evils.

Expert parallelism turns every MoE layer into an all-to-all communication problem: tokens shuffle to their expert's device and results shuffle back, and because the all-to-all is a barrier, load imbalance directly idles the whole cluster.

existing.name: moe-dense-vs-sparse-block
existing.improvement: Reads cleanly in both themes: the dense single-FFN path vs the router->E-experts->weighted-sum path is exactly the right first contrast, and highlighting only FFN 3 and FFN 17 as active makes k=2-of-E concrete. Only nitpick: the caption 'E total, only k=2 active (selected)' sits close under the expert row and just grazes the dispatch arrows - nudge it down a few px so it never touches the arrowheads. No redraw needed.

existing.name: moe-routing
existing.issue: Strong single-token walkthrough (tiny gate -> per-expert scores -> top-2 active -> weighted sum -> residual), but two things blur the softmax-then/topk-then distinction the chapter is careful about: (1) the per-expert 'routing score' numbers (0.05, 0.62, 0.07, 0.11, 0.38, 0.04) sum to ~1.27, yet 0.62 and 0.38 are ALSO shown as the renormalized top-2 weights that sum to 1 - the same two numbers doing double duty reads as if the raw scores already summed to 1; (2) the 'weighted sum' label overlaps the x0.38 combine curve, and the bottom caption grazes the FFN6 box.
existing.improvement: Make the raw scores clearly a slice of a full softmax (e.g. show them as small gray probabilities that visibly do NOT sum to 1) and then show the RENORMALIZE step producing distinct top-2 weights (relabel them so the reader sees 0.62/0.38 are computed FROM the scores, not equal to them) - this is the topk-then-softmax point. Nudge the 'weighted sum' label off the combine curve and lift the bottom caption clear of FFN6. Keep the overall layout.

existing.name: moe-router-dispatch-pipeline
existing.issue: Comprehensive but cluttered, and it overlaps heavily with moe-routing (same route->select->softmax->run->sum story) so it must earn its place by being the crisp end-to-end pipeline. Several labels overprint their own boxes/arrows in BOTH themes: 'non-differentiable step' sits on top of the top-k hexagon, 'rows sum to 1 (stable magnitude)' overlaps the softmax box, and 'run only experts {e1,...,ek}' collides with the container's top edge and the dashed weights arrow. The tiny 'indices {e1..ek}' text inside its pill is near-illegible. The dashed weights-routing arrows on the right add visual noise.
existing.improvement: De-clutter: move the annotations ('non-differentiable step', 'rows sum to 1', 'no gradient this step') to the OUTSIDE margin with short leader lines so they never sit on the shapes. Enlarge the 'indices'/'gate weights' pill text. This figure's unique value over moe-routing is (a) the optional noise/bias stage and (b) making the two outputs of top-k explicit: the INDICES (stop-gradient, used only to dispatch) vs the WEIGHTS (carry the gradient) - lean into that split and let moe-routing own the single-token intuition.

existing.name: moe-token-vs-expert-choice
existing.improvement: Excellent and legible in both themes: the side-by-side (tokens pick experts -> can overload -> drop, vs experts pick top-C tokens -> exactly C each -> balanced, some tokens chosen many times or none) nails the invert-the-problem idea, and the T5-unchosen / T3-chosen-twice asymmetry is a nice touch. Minor: the white 'overloaded -> drop' label overlaps the top edge of the E1 box - shift it up a few px so it clears the box outline. Otherwise no change.

existing.name: moe-dense-gshard-deepseek-lineage
existing.improvement: The evolutionary triptych (dense FFN -> GShard/Switch coarse routed -> DeepSeek fine-grained + always-on shared) is the right way to show the lineage and reads in both themes. Small fixes: the green 'always on' label in the DeepSeek panel overlaps the horizontal router line and the fine-grained expert row - nudge it clear; and the many small unlabeled fine-grained expert boxes could use a single '(mE small experts)' bracket so it's obvious they are the same experts, just more and smaller. Keep the layout and the per-panel footers (8x params/2x active; 256 routed + shared, top-8).

existing.name: moe-expert-parallel-all-to-all
existing.improvement: The best figure in the set: home GPUs -> all-to-all DISPATCH -> experts run locally -> all-to-all COMBINE, with tokens colored by destination GPU and the crossing arrows making the shuffle unmistakable; the 'GPU1: more tokens' hot-shard and the dashed 'all-to-all is a BARRIER... imbalance idles the entire cluster' footer tie architecture to throughput exactly as the chapter wants. Both themes read well. Only micro-nit: the 'chip color = dest. GPU' legend note sits close to GPU0's home tokens - a few px of breathing room is all it needs.

new_figures.name: moe-total-vs-active-resource-split
new_figures.concept: The sparsity dial made visual: E expert blocks = TOTAL params (capacity), only k highlighted = ACTIVE params (FLOPs), plus three resource meters showing quality~total, latency~active, memory~total.
new_figures.rationale: This is the chapter's central thesis and the entire Interview Corner ('reconcile 47B total / 13B active / 8x7B and why the RATIO, not a single number, is what you reason about'), yet nothing draws it - the existing figures show the routing mechanism but never the resource accounting. The single hardest misconception the chapter fights ('comparing MoE and dense by total parameters') is fixed by one picture that separates what capacity you HAVE (total) from what you PAY in compute (active) from what you PAY in memory (total). Foundational and currently unillustrated.
new_figures.placement: From One FFN to a Bank of Experts (after the 'Total parameters / Active parameters' paragraph ending '...so you want that ratio large.')
new_figures.brief: A single infographic in two coupled parts. LEFT (the model): draw a shared trunk box labeled 'shared: attention + embeddings + norms + head (counted once)', then a bank of E small FFN blocks (use E=8 illustrative) stacked/gridded, all the same size, bracketed as 'TOTAL params = shared + all E experts (capacity / knowledge)'. Highlight exactly k=2 of the E experts (glow/accent) and bracket 'shared + these k experts = ACTIVE params (what ONE token uses -> FLOPs/latency)'. Between them show the dial: 'total-to-active RATIO = E/k = 4x here (the sparsity dial)'. RIGHT (three resource meters, stacked): three horizontal bars/gauges each with a pointer, labeled: (1) 'QUALITY / capacity -> tracks TOTAL' with its fill reaching the full E-expert width; (2) 'LATENCY / FLOPs per token -> tracks ACTIVE' with its fill reaching only the k-expert width (annotate 'decodes like a small dense model'); (3) 'MEMORY / HBM -> tracks TOTAL' filled to full width (annotate 'all experts must stay resident: any token can route anywhere'). The punchline caption: 'you get TOTAL capacity at ACTIVE compute, but pay TOTAL memory - so compare MoE to dense by ACTIVE params or by FLOPs, never by total.' Use ONLY small illustrative integers (E=8, k=2, ratio 4x); do NOT print benchmark param counts (no 47B/13B) - keep it schematic so it generalizes. Theme-safe: shared trunk and inactive experts in a neutral fill, active experts in a single theme-safe accent, meter fills as accent-on-track; all text in theme foreground; strict left-to-right reading order, everything inside the viewBox.

new_figures.name: moe-router-collapse-and-aux-loss
new_figures.concept: The rich-get-richer collapse feedback loop that kills naive MoE, and how the auxiliary loss breaks it by multiplying a hard (non-diff) dispatch count as DIRECTION by a soft (differentiable) probability as GRADIENT.
new_figures.rationale: 'Load Balancing: The Make-or-Break Problem' is called the difference between MoE working and failing, and it is the most conceptually subtle section (the hard-count x soft-prob trick is 'why MoE trains at all'), yet it has NO figure. The collapse feedback loop and the direction/gradient decomposition of the aux loss are exactly the kind of mechanism a diagram conveys far better than prose. This is the biggest genuine conceptual gap in the chapter.
new_figures.placement: Load Balancing: The Make-or-Break Problem (after the opening rich-get-richer paragraph, before 'The auxiliary load-balancing loss')
new_figures.brief: Two stacked panels. TOP PANEL - THE COLLAPSE (the problem): draw a closed feedback CYCLE of 4 nodes with arrows forming a loop: 'one expert is slightly better (random init)' -> 'router sends it MORE tokens' -> 'it gets MORE gradient' -> 'it improves FASTER' -> (back to start) with a bold '+' / 'reinforces' marker on the loop to signal a runaway positive-feedback cycle. To the right of the cycle, a small before/after pair of expert-load HISTOGRAMS over E=8 experts: 'early: roughly uniform' (flat bars) --arrow labeled 'left unchecked'--> 'collapsed: one tall bar, the rest starved (no gradient, atrophy)'. Caption: 'you paid for E experts and trained one; in EP the hot expert's GPU saturates while others idle.' BOTTOM PANEL - THE FIX (aux loss): show the loss L_aux = alpha * E * sum_e (f_e * P_e) with the product visually decomposed: over the SAME collapsed histogram, draw f_e as HARD dispatch counts tagged 'hard count -> DIRECTION (which experts are overloaded); non-differentiable, treated as constant (detached)' and P_e as a soft probability curve tagged 'soft mean router prob -> GRADIENT (nudges router down on overloaded, up on starved); differentiable'. Draw a small 'x' between them and an arrow to a corrective force pushing the tall bar DOWN and the starved bars UP toward the uniform 1/E line (draw a dashed 'uniform 1/E target' line across the histogram). One-line moral: 'the loss READS imbalance from the hard counts and WRITES the correction through the soft probabilities.' Optionally a tiny footnote chip: 'expert-choice routing avoids this by construction (each expert picks its top-C tokens).' Use small illustrative integers (E=8); invent no numbers. Theme-safe: neutral bars with a single accent for the overloaded expert and the corrective arrows; the uniform target as a dashed neutral line; all labels in theme foreground with leader lines so nothing overprints the bars; clear top-then-bottom reading order, everything within the viewBox.

new_figures.name: moe-capacity-buffer-drop-pad
new_figures.concept: Capacity as a fixed-size rectangular buffer per expert: capacity = ceil(Cf * Nk/E); tokens above the line are dropped to the residual, empty slots below are zero-padded - visualizing the drop-vs-pad tradeoff the capacity factor tunes.
new_figures.rationale: The 'Capacity Factor, Token Dropping, and the Worked Numbers' section is entirely textual (formula + worked example) with no figure, yet the core intuition - 'GPUs want rectangular tensors, so each expert gets a fixed buffer; overflow drops, underflow pads' - is inherently visual and easy to misread from prose. A single picture of three expert buffers (one overflowing/dropping, one underfilled/padding, one just right) makes the Cf tradeoff and the ceil(Cf*Nk/E) formula concrete, and connects to the batch-dependence pitfall and to serving's dropless-GEMM alternative.
new_figures.placement: Capacity Factor, Token Dropping, and the Worked Numbers (after the capacity = ceil(Cf * Nk/E) formula, near the worked example)
new_figures.brief: Center the layout on three side-by-side EXPERT BUFFERS drawn as tall rectangular columns of equal height, each divided into token slots. Draw a horizontal dashed line across all three at the same height labeled 'capacity = ceil(Cf * Nk/E) (fixed buffer height; GPUs need rectangular tensors)'; also mark a fainter line at the average load 'Nk/E' below it so the reader sees capacity = Cf x average with 'Cf slack for imbalance' as the gap. EXPERT A (overloaded): filled ABOVE the capacity line, with the overflow tokens drawn spilling OUT past the line, greyed and tagged 'DROPPED -> skip FFN, pass through on the residual (quality cost)'. EXPERT B (underfilled): filled only partway, the empty slots up to the line hatched/ghosted and tagged 'PADDED with zeros -> wasted FLOPs (compute cost)'. EXPERT C (just right): filled exactly to the line, tagged 'ideal: full, no drop, no pad'. Above the three, a small router fan-in showing tokens being dispatched (uneven arrow thickness A>C>B) to convey that imbalance is what causes A to overflow and B to starve. Footer with the two knobs: 'Cf too LOW -> many drops (hurts loss); Cf too HIGH -> more padding (wastes compute); typical Cf in [1.0, 2.0]' and a chip 'dropless MoE (Megablocks grouped-GEMM) handles ragged sizes -> no drop, no pad'. Use small illustrative integers only (e.g. show a handful of slots per buffer; do NOT invent the 8192/2560 worked-example numbers - keep it schematic). Theme-safe: neutral slot fills, a single accent for dropped-overflow tokens, hatched/ghosted neutral for padding, dashed neutral capacity line; all labels in theme foreground with leaders; clear reading order, everything inside the viewBox.

summary: This chapter is figure-rich (6 existing) and its systems/mechanism side is well covered: moe-dense-vs-sparse-block, moe-token-vs-expert-choice, moe-dense-gshard-deepseek-lineage, and especially moe-expert-parallel-all-to-all are strong keeps that read in both themes. Two existing figures need refines: moe-router-dispatch-pipeline has real label-on-shape overprints in both themes and overlaps moe-routing (it should specialize into the indices-vs-weights split), and moe-routing blurs the topk-then-softmax renormalization by having the same two numbers serve as both raw scores and final weights. The genuine CONCEPTUAL gaps are the chapter's own headline ideas that no figure draws: (1) the total-vs-active resource accounting - quality~total, latency~active, memory~total - which is the entire thesis and Interview Corner (priority-1 new figure); (2) the rich-get-richer collapse feedback loop and the aux loss's hard-count(direction) x soft-prob(gradient) trick, the 'make-or-break' mechanism with zero visuals (priority-1); and (3) capacity as a rectangular drop-vs-pad buffer, currently text-only (priority-2). These three make the economic bet, the training-stability mechanism, and the systems buffer intuition click, respectively.
